01. Random experiments-Definitions
Randomexperiment:aprocessleadingtotwoormorepossibleoutcomes,
withoutknowingexactlywhichoutcomewilloccur.
Sample space (푆or Ω): the collection of all possible basic outcomes of a random 
experiment.
•Throwing a dice →S=1,2,3,4,5,6
Event:any subset of basic outcomes from the Sample space.
•“Less than 3” →퐴=1,2
•“Lower than 10” →퐵=푆
•“Higher than 8” →퐶=∅(empty set, the set that contains no elements)
•“Between 2.5 and 3.5” → 퐸={3} (each single element in the Sample space is 
called basic element)
Probability: measure of the uncertainty of an event

5
01. Random experiments -Definitions
Null event(∅). It represents the absence of a basic outcome from the sample space. 
e.g.,  Random experiment: Throwing a dice →푆=1,2,3,4,5,6
“Higher than 8” →∅
Intersection퐴∩퐵of two events 퐴and 퐵. It is the set of all outcomes that belong to both 퐴
and 퐵.
e.g., Random experiment: Throwing a dice →S=1,2,3,4,5,6
퐴:=“even number” =2,4,6; 퐵=:“greater than 4” =5,6; A∩퐵=6
Union 퐴∪퐵of two events 퐴and 퐵. It is the set of all outcomes in  푆that belong to either 퐴
or 퐵.
e.g., Random experiment: Throwing a dice →S=1,2,3,4,5,6
퐴:=“even number” =2,4,6; 퐵:=“greater than 4” =5,6; A∪퐵=2,4,5,6
Complement (
ҧ
퐴) of an event 퐴. It is the set of all basic outcomes in 푆that do not belong to 퐴.
e.g., Random experiment: Throwing a dice →S=1,2,3,4,5,6
퐴:=“even number” =2,4,6;
ҧ
퐴:=1,3,5
(Note that sometimes the complement is written as 
ഥ
A, A
c
, or ¬A)

6
01. Random experiments -Definitions
Two events 퐴and 퐵are Mutually Exclusive, or Incompatible, if 퐴∩퐵=∅. 
e.g.,  Random experiment: Throwing a dice →S=1,2,3,4,5,6
퐴=“even number” =2,4,6; 퐵=1,3; A∩퐵=∅
Two or more events are Collectively exhaustiveif their union is the sample space S.
e.g., Random experiment: Throwing a dice →S=1,2,3,4,5,6
퐴=“even number” =2,4,6; 퐵=“lower than 4” =1,2,3;
퐶=“greater than 2”=3,4,5,6; 퐴∪퐵∪퐶=1,2,3,4,5,6=푆.
Two or more events are Mutually Exclusive and Collectively exhaustiveif their 
intersection is ∅and  their union is the sample space S.
e.g., Random experiment: Throwing a dice →S=1,2,3,4,5,6
퐴=“even number” =2,4,6; 퐵=1,3andC={5}
A collection of events that is mutually exclusive and collectively 
exhaustive forms a partition of the sample space.
S

Exercise. Let 푆be the sample space of all possible outcomes of a satisfaction 
survey, S=1,2,3,4,5,being 1=“very unsatisfied” and 5=“very satisfied”.
Let 퐴≔Answer is not “very satisfied”={1,2,3,4}and 퐵:=Answer is “satisfied or 
very satisfied”={4,5}
a)Calculate 
ҧ
퐴and 
ത
퐵.
b)Calculate 퐴∩퐵and 퐴∪퐵.
c)Are 퐴and 퐵mutually exclusive? Are they collectively exhaustive?
ҧ
퐴=5, 
ത
퐵=1,2,3
퐴∩퐵=4, 퐴∪퐵=1,2,3,4,5=푆
Since 퐴∩퐵=4≠∅, 퐴and 퐵are not mutually exclusive.
Since 1,2,3,4,5=S, 퐴and 퐵are collectively exhaustive.

01. Random experiments -Exercise
According to The World Bank data, there are 218 countries* in the world:
•37 countries in the Asia-Pacific Region (A)
•49 countries with more than 30 million inhabitants (B)
•9 countries in the Asia-Pacific Region have more than 30 million inhabitants.
Let’s suppose we pick a country randomly. 
What’s the Venn diagram 
corresponding to ...
i.Countries from the Asia-Pacific region 
ii.Countries fromthe Asia-Pacific region 
with more than 30 million inhabitants
iii.Countries with more than 30 million 
inhabitants or from the Asia-Pacific 
region
iv.Countries with more than 30 million 
inhabitants but not fromthe Asia-Pacific 
region
1
1
23
456
789
6
3
8
*Source: https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.POP.TOTL

02. Probability definition
Probability:Chancethatagiveneventwilloccur(alwaysbetween0and1)
Methods to calculate probability:
•Classical probability 
•Relative frequency probability
•Subjective probability
Methods to define probability:
•Probability postulates
0≤푃퐴≤1,foranyevent퐴.

푃(퐴)=

Conditions to use it:
•All outcomes in the sample space are equally likely to occur.
•Requires a count of the outcomes in the sample space (finite number of 
outcomes).

02. Probability definition
DefinitionExample
Subjective
•Subjective probability expresses an individual’s degree of belief about 
the chance that an event will occur.
•Is based on previous experience, personal opinionand/or intuition
•Subjective probabilities are used in certain management decision 
procedures.
Basedonpreviousexperienceamanagermight
estimatethattheprobabilitythatsalesofacertain
productmightincreaseinthenewcampaignis
about70%

03. Probability rules -Postulates
For each event 퐴⊆S, the probability of occurrence, 푃퐴, is set according to the following 
postulates:

03. Probability rules –Addition rule
Addition rule →푃퐴∪퐵=푃퐴+푃퐵–푃퐴∩퐵
퐴∩퐵

We subtract the probability of the intersection 
of events, 푃퐴∩퐵, to avoid double counting.

03. Probability rules –Addition rule
It is known that in a High School:
•60% of students play football, 
•38% play basketball, 
•40% go cycling. 
Besides, we know that:
•those who play football and basketball are 16%, 
•those who play football and cycling are 20%, 
•those who play basketball and cycling are 15% and finally, 
•those who practice all three sports are 3%. 
0.12

•The probability of randomly choosing a student who practices at least one sport
Calculate:
•The probability of randomly choosing a student who does not play basketball
•The probability of randomly choosing a student who plays football or basketball
